title: Add flag --cert-dir and --tls-verify to kpod login
body:
This commit adds a mechanism to override the default certs dir by using
command line flag `--cert-dir` for kpod login.

Another flag `--tls-verify` is also added which lets you skip certificate
validation when contacting container registry.

Signed-off-by: Suraj Deshmukh <surajd.service@gmail.com>

Fixes #38

comments.body:
Can one of the admins verify this patch?
I understand the following commands:
- `bot, add author to whitelist`
- `bot, test pull request`
- `bot, test pull request once`
bot, add author to whitelist
Should we allow this to be overridden via an option in kpod?  For testing purposes?
@mheon PTAL

@rhatdan I think this default location should be overwritten if the --certs-dir paramenter is provided at the command line with the kpod command.  However we probably want to review the commands that do and don't have that parameter and then add (or maybe remove) as necessary.
@TomSweeneyRedHat do you suggest I add a cmd line parameter to over ride the default we setup here? Do you want me to solve https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/issues/38 in the same PR?

@surajssd I think @umohnani8 is in process on #38.  I'd not add a command line param for the PR to solve this issue.  I think for now creating a default directory for the certs that is used by the kpod commands is sufficient.
@TomSweeneyRedHat I haven't gotten started on #38 yet. Feel free to incorporate #38 into this PR if you want to @surajssd.
@surajssd Coul you look into allowing the --cirtdir be specified and add to this PR>
@surajssd, @rhatdan had a slight typo in his latest.  The parameter should be "--cert-dir"
@umohnani8 thank you. I am working on it now, but also I think this PR needs more than just changing the directory.
So I realized that setting `OCICertPath` has no effect on the behavior, only changes made to `DockerCertPath` are reflected.

> So I realized that setting `OCICertPath` has no effect on the behavior, only changes made to `DockerCertPath` are reflected.

`OCICertPath` is used for fetching foreign layers for `oci*:` transports, which are otherwise local.

---

Again, _please_ start with exactly describing, in English, what it is that you want this PR to do.
@mtrmac I have tried my best to explain what this PR does, see if there are any problems that you see, I will fix those as well.

@surajssd All of the explanation is “Change the default CA cert directory to …”

For a start:

- Why is anything changing? What is the benefit?
- The description says ”change the default”; the code is instead looping through alternatives.
- The default CA cert directory used by what? Only `docker://`? A particular set of containers/image transports? Every single thing that uses TLS within `kpod`?
- The description says “CA cert directory”; `DockerCertPath` actually changes not only CAs, but also sets up client certificates and client private keys. Is that intended or not?
- Why `kpod login` only? If `pull`/`push` don’t use the same CA configuration, the information stored by `login` can’t be used; so what is the point?
  - And because the CA configuration needs to be shared for all these commands, is it ever really useful to have it in a CLI instead of primarily in a config file? Shouldn’t the config file come first? Or even better, shouldn’t there be a global default that works without a config file?
- Should only the `kpod` CLI use this directory, or also other tools like `skopeo` and the whole CRI-O runtime?
- To the extent this overrides the `/etc/docker/certs.d` default behavior of containers/image, should that be completely overridden and never used? Or should both be tried?

---

I _guess_ that `/etc/containers/certs.d` should be added as a path used by default in c/image/docker, with using the first match (whatever first means), and that `kpod login` should not be trying several dirs in turn, or really configuring anything by default. But that really depends on what the goal is in the first place.

> To the extent this overrides the /etc/docker/certs.d default behavior of containers/image, should that be completely overridden and never used? Or should both be tried?

Or should `/etc/containers/certs.d` be always used if it exists, ignoring whatever is in `/etc/docker/certs.d`?
@mtrmac, @surajssd is trying to solve issue #39

and I hit comment by accident.  In short, currently the default certificate directory that kpod * uses is the one provided by Docker.  The issue/PR is trying to move that to a new default location.  If you've more concerns or thoughts, I'm happy to hear them, but perhaps it would be best done in the issue or perhaps as a post scrum when @rhatdan returns?

That still leaves the questions in https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/pull/75#issuecomment-348238097 unaddressed I think.

(I can give up on the ”benefit” one :) , that one exists only because having the end goal in mind should make it easier to answer the other ones.)

@surajssd All of the explanation is “Change the default CA cert directory to …”

For a start:

    Why is anything changing? What is the benefit?
`DAN: Main benefit is not being tied to Docker, and also allowing flexibility for testing.`
    The description says ”change the default”; the code is instead looping through alternatives
`
DAN: Probably should be allow changing the location of the Certs directory.  By default we will search two directories for certs.  If overridden, we will only search the specified location.
`.
    The default CA cert directory used by what? Only docker://? A particular set of containers/image transports? Every single thing that uses TLS within kpod?
    The description says “CA cert directory”; DockerCertPath actually changes not only CAs, but also sets up client certificates and client private keys. Is that intended or not?
    Why kpod login only? If pull/push don’t use the same CA configuration, the information stored by login can’t be used; so what is the point?
`DAN: Yes all kpod commands that use containers/image should be able to use this option, pull, push, run. create.`
        And because the CA configuration needs to be shared for all these commands, is it ever really useful to have it in a CLI instead of primarily in a config file? Shouldn’t the config file come first? Or even better, shouldn’t there be a global default that works without a config file?
`DAN: Trying to avoid config files for now, If we want a config file it should be part of containers/image not kpod, so that tools like cri-o, buildah, skopeo etc can use it.`

    Should only the kpod CLI use this directory, or also other tools like skopeo and the whole CRI-O runtime?
`DAN:  Yes if we are going to move to a config file as part of containers/image`

    To the extent this overrides the /etc/docker/certs.d default behavior of containers/image, should that be completely overridden and never used? Or should both be tried?
`DAN: I think we need to use both, just like we do with the login information`

I guess that /etc/containers/certs.d should be added as a path used by default in c/image/docker, with using the first match (whatever first means), and that kpod login should not be trying several dirs in turn, or really configuring anything by default. But that really depends on what the goal is in the first place.
`DAN: Yes I agree`
@surajssd Can you rebase this to pick up the gitvalidation fix for the tests?
LGTM

> > I _guess_ that `/etc/containers/certs.d` should be added as a path used by default in `c/image/docker`, with using the first match (whatever first means), and that `kpod login` should not be trying several dirs in turn, or really configuring anything by default. But that really depends on what the goal is in the first place.
>
> DAN: Yes I agree

OK; then:
- That `for _, dir := range certDirs` loop should not exist in `kpod` at all.
- `kpod` should only set `SystemContext.DockerCertPath` if the user explicitly uses a command-line option.
- The default `/etc/containers/certs.d; /etc/docker/certs.d` path pair should be implemented in `containers/image/docker.dockerCertDir`.

@TomSweeneyRedHat I might need some pointers with setting up tests in this case, I was following this to try out the local registry but this seems to be failing for some reason.

Something that works with docker:


Generating certs as mentioned in https://docs.docker.com/registry/insecure/#use-self-signed-certificates

```console
$ openssl req   -newkey rsa:4096 -nodes -sha256 -keyout certs/domain.key   -x509 -days 365 -out certs/domain.crt
Generating a 4096 bit RSA private key
.....................................................................................................................++
..........................................................................................................................................++
writing new private key to 'certs/domain.key'
-----
You are about to be asked to enter information that will be incorporated
into your certificate request.
What you are about to enter is what is called a Distinguished Name or a DN.
There are quite a few fields but you can leave some blank
For some fields there will be a default value,
If you enter '.', the field will be left blank.
-----
Country Name (2 letter code) [XX]:IN
State or Province Name (full name) []:KA
Locality Name (eg, city) [Default City]:Bangalore
Organization Name (eg, company) [Default Company Ltd]:Red Hat
Organizational Unit Name (eg, section) []:
Common Name (eg, your name or your server's hostname) []:localhost
Email Address []:foo@bar.com

$ ll certs/
total 8
-rw-rw-r--. 1 vagrant vagrant 2041 Dec  5 05:02 domain.crt
-rw-------. 1 vagrant vagrant 3272 Dec  5 05:01 domain.key
```

Using those certs to run the registry

```console
docker run -d \
  --restart=always \
  --name registry \
  -v `pwd`/certs:/certs:Z \
  -e REGISTRY_HTTP_TLS_CERTIFICATE=/certs/domain.crt \
  -e REGISTRY_HTTP_TLS_KEY=/certs/domain.key \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  registry:2

```

Moving those certs to the dir

```console
$ sudo mkdir /etc/docker/certs.d/localhost:5000
$ sudo cp ./certs/domain.crt /etc/docker/certs.d/localhost\:5000/ca.crt
```

Now using those certs to login

```console
$ kpod --log-level  debug login -u surajd@redhat.com -p fhsahJuAicB3lL6pZO8ryw localhost:5000  
DEBU[0000] Looking for TLS certificates and private keys in /etc/containers/certs.d/localhost:5000                                 
DEBU[0000] GET https://localhost:5000/v2/                                                      
DEBU[0000] Ping https://localhost:5000/v2/ err &url.Error{Op:"Get", URL:"https://localhost:5000/v2/", Err:x509.UnknownAuthorityError{Cert:(*x509.Certificate)(0xc420428f00), hintErr:error(nil), hintCert:(*x509.Certificate)(nil)}}
DEBU[0000] GET https://localhost:5000/v1_ping
DEBU[0000] Ping https://localhost:5000/v1/_ping err &url.Error{Op:"Get", URL:"https://localhost:5000/v1/_ping", Err:x509.UnknownAuthorityError{Cert:(*x509.Certificate)(0xc420732000), hintErr:error(nil), hintCert:(*x509.Certificate)(nil)}}
DEBU[0000] Looking for TLS certificates and private keys in /etc/docker/certs.d/localhost:5000
DEBU[0000]  crt: /etc/docker/certs.d/localhost:5000/ca.crt                         
DEBU[0000] GET https://localhost:5000/v2/

DEBU[0000] Ping https://localhost:5000/v2/ status 200
DEBU[0000] GET https://localhost:5000/v2/
Login Succeeded!
```

@surajssd Can you open a PR for containers/image rather then here like @mtrmac suggested.

@surajssd on the tests - you need to provide a command to kpod run for now. Docker will let you run with only an image, and will default to the image's ENTRYPOINT or COMMAND if no command is specified, but we haven't implemented that in kpod yet.
@rhatdan @mtrmac created a PR upstream https://github.com/containers/image/pull/388
About this PR should we just solve issue https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/issues/38 with this PR? And get back to #39 whenever upstream is fixed?
Yes makes sense.

I tried writing tests for this PR like following:

```diff
diff --git a/test/helpers.bash b/test/helpers.bash
index 2117716e..22c1efa5 100644
--- a/test/helpers.bash
+++ b/test/helpers.bash
@@ -65,6 +65,10 @@ fi
 
 TESTDIR=$(mktemp -p /test.dir -d)
 
+# Path to the certs that will be generated for registry
+REG_CERTS_DIR="${INTEGRATION_ROOT}/certs"
+REG_AUTH_DIR="${INTEGRATION_ROOT}/auth"
+
 declare -A -g IMAGES
 IMAGES+=(["alpine"]=docker.io/library/alpine:latest ["busybox"]=docker.io/library/busybox:latest)
 
@@ -309,3 +313,23 @@ function copy_images() {
         "$COPYIMG_BINARY" --root "$TESTDIR/crio" $STORAGE_OPTIONS --runroot "$TESTDIR/crio-run" --image-name=${IMAGES[${key}]} --import-from=dir:"$ARTIFACTS_PATH"/${key} --add-name=${IMAGES[${key}]}
     done
 }
+
+function generate_registry_data() {
+    mkdir -p ${REG_CERTS_DIR}
+    openssl req -newkey rsa:4096 -nodes -sha256 -keyout ${REG_CERTS_DIR}/domain.key -x509 -days 365 -out ${REG_CERTS_DIR}/domain.crt -subj "/C=IN/ST=KA/L=BLR/O=RHT/OU=IT/CN=localhost"
+
+    mkdir -p ${REG_AUTH_DIR}
+    cat >${REG_AUTH_DIR}/htpasswd <<-EOF
+user:$2y$05$Q23j/DE9Sl.A0q8XRd4NVeGcKGE6u7doiEOoU80jVuqYvf2wtaTGi
+
+EOF
+}
+
+function clean_registry_data() {
+    rm -rf ${REG_CERTS_DIR} ${REG_AUTH_DIR}
+}
+
+function start_registry() {
+    run bash -c "${KPOD_BINARY} ${KPOD_OPTIONS} run -d --name registry -v ${REG_AUTH_DIR}:/auth:Z -e REGISTRY_AUTH=htpasswd -e 'REGISTRY_AUTH_HTPASSWD_REALM=Registry Realm' -e REGISTRY_AUTH_HTPASSWD_PATH=/auth/htpasswd -v ${REG_CERTS_DIR}:/certs:Z -e REGISTRY_HTTP_TLS_CERTIFICATE=/certs/domain.crt -e REGISTRY_HTTP_TLS_KEY=/certs/domain.key -p 5000:5000 docker.io/registry:2 /entrypoint.sh /etc/docker/registry/config.yml"
+    echo "$output"
+}
diff --git a/test/kpod_login.bats b/test/kpod_login.bats
new file mode 100644
index 00000000..40ee08e3
--- /dev/null
+++ b/test/kpod_login.bats
@@ -0,0 +1,19 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env bats
+
+load helpers
+
+function setup() {
+    generate_registry_data
+    start_registry
+}
+
+function teardown() {
+    cleanup_test
+    clean_registry_data
+}
+
+@test "kpod login with username and password certs don't exist" {
+	run ${KPOD_BINARY} ${KPOD_OPTIONS} login -u user -p password --tls-verify=false localhost:5000
+	echo "$output"
+	[ "$status" -eq 0 ]
+}
\ No newline at end of file
```
